expanded_description: Triangles are classified in two independent ways: by their side lengths (scalene, isosceles, equilateral) and by their angle measures (acute, right, obtuse, equiangular). These classifications provide the fundamental vocabulary for all triangle geometry and determine which theorems and properties apply. Understanding both classification systems and how they interact is the essential first step in the study of triangles.

core_concept.principle: Every triangle belongs to exactly one side-length category and exactly one angle-measure category. Side classification: scalene (no congruent sides), isosceles (at least two congruent sides), equilateral (three congruent sides). Angle classification: acute (all angles < 90°), right (one 90° angle), obtuse (one angle > 90°), equiangular (all angles = 60°). These classifications are independent but related—equilateral triangles are always equiangular, and vice versa.
core_concept.explanation: Think of triangle classification like describing a person—you can describe their height (side classification) and their hair color (angle classification) independently. A triangle might be 'isosceles' (like having two matching features) and also 'right' (having a 90° corner). Some combinations are possible (isosceles right triangle), while others are guaranteed together (equilateral means equiangular). Learning these categories gives you the vocabulary to describe any triangle precisely and to know which theorems apply—isosceles triangles have special base angle properties, right triangles have the Pythagorean Theorem, and equilateral triangles have all the symmetries.

image.alt: A central triangle diagram with two classification branches. Left branch: Side Classification — scalene (all sides different, no tick marks), isosceles (two sides with matching ticks), equilateral (all three sides with matching ticks). Right branch: Angle Classification — acute (all angles < 90°), right (one angle with square mark), obtuse (one angle > 90°), equiangular (all angles marked 60°). Arrows show equilateral ↔ equiangular.
image.caption: Triangle classification: by sides (scalene, isosceles, equilateral) and by angles (acute, right, obtuse, equiangular).

sections.introduction: The most fundamental way to describe a triangle is by comparing the lengths of its three sides. This classification—scalene, isosceles, or equilateral—determines the triangle's symmetry properties and which congruence theorems apply. The side classification is based on the number of congruent sides.

introduction_image.alt: Three triangles side by side: (1) Scalene — all three sides with different tick marks, no sides equal. (2) Isosceles — two sides with matching single ticks, third side with double tick. (3) Equilateral — all three sides with matching single ticks. Each labeled with the classification name and the defining condition.
introduction_image.caption: Side classification: scalene (no equal sides), isosceles (at least two equal sides), equilateral (three equal sides).

key_concept.definition: Triangles are classified by the number of congruent sides: Scalene triangle — no congruent sides (all three sides have different lengths). Isosceles triangle — at least two congruent sides (the congruent sides are called legs, the third side is the base). Equilateral triangle — all three sides congruent. Equilateral triangles are a special case of isosceles triangles.
key_concept.context: Side classification determines which theorems apply. Isosceles triangles have the Isosceles Triangle Theorem (base angles are congruent). Equilateral triangles have additional properties (all angles 60°, all altitudes are also medians and angle bisectors). Scalene triangles have no special side-based symmetries.

image.alt: Three triangles with detailed labels: (1) Scalene — sides $a$, $b$, $c$, all $\neq$. (2) Isosceles — legs marked with matching ticks, base marked differently. Vertex angle at top, base angles at bottom. Label: 'Legs = congruent sides, Base = third side.' (3) Equilateral — all sides matching ticks, label: 'All sides $\cong$.'
image.caption: Side classification: scalene (0 congruent sides), isosceles (≥2), equilateral (3).

classifications.value: A triangle with no congruent sides. All three sides have different lengths.
characteristics: No two sides are equal: $AB \neq BC \neq CA$
No special symmetry properties
All three angles are also different (by the converse of the Isosceles Triangle Theorem)
Most general type of triangle — no restrictions on side lengths other than the Triangle Inequality
classifications.behavior: Scalene triangles are the 'default' triangle. Most randomly drawn triangles are scalene. They have no guaranteed equal angles or symmetry lines. The Triangle Inequality is the only constraint on their side lengths.

examples.explanation:
Triangle with sides $5$, $7$, and $9$ units.

$5 eq 7$, $7 eq 9$, $5 eq 9$ → no congruent sides → scalene.

All three angles will also be different: approximately $33.6°$, $50.7°$, $95.7°$.

image.alt: A scalene triangle with sides labeled 5, 7, 9. No tick marks (all different). Angles shown with different arcs (no matching). Label: 'Scalene — no equal sides, no equal angles.'
image.caption: Scalene triangle: all sides different, all angles different.

classifications.value: A triangle with at least two congruent sides. The congruent sides are called legs; the third side is the base.
characteristics: At least two sides congruent: $AB = AC$ (legs), $BC$ is the base
The angles opposite the congruent sides are congruent (Base Angles Theorem)
The angle between the legs is the vertex angle; the angles at the base are base angles
Has a line of symmetry through the vertex angle that bisects the base
classifications.behavior: Isosceles triangles are the most commonly used special triangle in proofs. The Isosceles Triangle Theorem (if two sides are congruent, the angles opposite them are congruent) and its converse are fundamental results. An equilateral triangle is a special isosceles triangle where all three sides happen to be equal.

examples.explanation:
Isosceles $\triangle ABC$ with $AB = AC$.

Legs: $AB$ and $AC$ (the congruent sides).
Base: $BC$ (the third side).
Vertex angle: $\angle A$ (between the legs).
Base angles: $\angle B$ and $\angle C$ (opposite the legs).

By the Isosceles Triangle Theorem: $\angle B \cong \angle C$.

image.alt: An isosceles triangle with legs $AB$ and $AC$ marked with matching tick marks. Base $BC$ marked differently. Vertex angle at $A$ labeled. Base angles $\angle B$ and $\angle C$ marked with matching arcs and labeled congruent. Line of symmetry shown as dashed line from $A$ to midpoint of $BC$.
image.caption: Isosceles triangle: legs congruent, base angles congruent, line of symmetry.

classifications.value: A triangle with all three sides congruent. It is a special case of an isosceles triangle.
characteristics: All three sides congruent: $AB = BC = CA$
All three angles congruent: $m\angle A = m\angle B = m\angle C = 60°$
Equilateral $\leftrightarrow$ Equiangular (each implies the other)
Has three lines of symmetry; every altitude is also a median and angle bisector
classifications.behavior: Equilateral triangles are the most symmetric triangles. All properties of isosceles triangles apply (from any vertex). The equilateral/equiangular equivalence is a fundamental theorem: a triangle is equilateral if and only if it is equiangular.

examples.explanation:
$\triangle ABC$ with $AB = BC = CA = 6$ units.

Since all sides are equal, all angles are equal: $m\angle A = m\angle B = m\angle C = 60°$.

The triangle is both equilateral (by sides) and equiangular (by angles).

Any altitude divides the triangle into two congruent $30°-60°-90°$ triangles.

image.alt: An equilateral triangle with all three sides marked with matching tick marks. All three angles marked 60° with matching arcs. Three lines of symmetry shown as dashed lines from each vertex to the opposite side's midpoint. Label: 'Equilateral = Equiangular. All angles 60°.'
image.caption: Equilateral triangle: three equal sides, three 60° angles, three lines of symmetry.

image.alt: Complete side classification reference: three triangles displayed with a summary table. Scalene: 0 congruent sides. Isosceles: ≥2 congruent sides (legs, base, vertex angle, base angles labeled). Equilateral: 3 congruent sides (special isosceles). Each with key properties listed.
image.caption: Triangle Classification by Sides: scalene, isosceles, and equilateral with their defining properties.

sections.introduction: Triangles can also be classified by their largest angle. An acute triangle has all angles less than 90°, a right triangle has one 90° angle, and an obtuse triangle has one angle greater than 90°. The angle classification determines which theorems (like the Pythagorean Theorem for right triangles) apply and places constraints on the possible side lengths.

introduction_image.alt: Four triangles: (1) Acute — all three angles < 90° (marked 50°, 60°, 70°). (2) Right — one angle with square mark (90°), other two acute. (3) Obtuse — one angle > 90° (marked 100°), other two acute. (4) Equiangular — all angles marked 60° with matching arcs. Each labeled with classification and angle sum verification (180°).
introduction_image.caption: Angle classification: acute (all < 90°), right (one = 90°), obtuse (one > 90°), equiangular (all = 60°).

key_concept.definition: Triangles are classified by their angle measures: Acute triangle — all three angles are acute ($< 90°$). Right triangle — exactly one right angle ($= 90°$); the other two are acute and complementary. Obtuse triangle — exactly one obtuse angle ($> 90°$); the other two are acute. Equiangular triangle — all three angles are congruent (each $60°$). The Triangle Angle Sum Theorem ($180°$) guarantees at most one right or obtuse angle.
key_concept.context: Angle classification is essential for the Pythagorean Theorem (right triangles only), trigonometric ratios, and the relationships between sides and angles. The largest angle is always opposite the longest side, connecting the side and angle classification systems.

image.alt: Four triangles with angle measures displayed: (1) Acute: 50°, 60°, 70° — all < 90°. (2) Right: 90° (square), 35°, 55° — note 35°+55°=90° complementary. (3) Obtuse: 100°, 40°, 40° — one > 90°. (4) Equiangular: 60°, 60°, 60° — all equal. Each with the condition highlighted.
image.caption: Angle classification: acute, right, obtuse, and equiangular. Exactly one right or obtuse angle possible.

classifications.value: A triangle in which all three interior angles are acute (less than 90°).
characteristics: $m\angle A < 90°$, $m\angle B < 90°$, $m\angle C < 90°$
All three angles are less than 90°
The triangle can be scalene, isosceles, or equilateral
Most triangles (in a random sense) are acute
classifications.behavior: In an acute triangle, all altitudes lie inside the triangle, and the orthocenter (intersection of altitudes) is inside. The Pythagorean relationship gives $a^2 + b^2 > c^2$ for all sides if the triangle is acute (where $c$ is the longest side).

examples.explanation:
Triangle with angles $55°$, $60°$, $65°$.

All three $< 90°$ → acute triangle.

Sum: $55° + 60° + 65° = 180°$ ✓.

This triangle happens to be scalene (all angles different → all sides different).

image.alt: An acute triangle with angles 55°, 60°, 65° labeled. All angle arcs are acute (< 90°). A note: 'All angles < 90° → Acute. Can be scalene, isosceles, or equilateral.'
image.caption: Acute triangle: all three angles less than 90°.

classifications.value: A triangle with exactly one right angle (90°). The side opposite the right angle is the hypotenuse; the other two sides are legs.
characteristics: Exactly one angle $= 90°$ (marked with a square corner symbol)
The other two angles are acute and complementary (sum to $90°$)
The hypotenuse is the longest side (opposite the right angle)
Pythagorean Theorem: $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ where $c$ is the hypotenuse
classifications.behavior: Right triangles are the foundation of trigonometry. The Pythagorean Theorem relates the three sides. Right triangles can be scalene (e.g., 3-4-5) or isosceles (45°-45°-90°). A right triangle cannot be equilateral or obtuse.

examples.process: Identifying parts of a right triangle
examples.explanation:
Right $\triangle ABC$ with right angle at $C$ ($m\angle C = 90°$).

Hypotenuse: $AB$ (opposite the right angle, longest side).
Legs: $AC$ and $BC$.

$m\angle A + m\angle B = 90°$ (complementary acute angles).

Example: 3-4-5 right triangle — legs 3 and 4, hypotenuse 5.

image.alt: A right triangle with right angle at C marked with square. Hypotenuse AB labeled as longest side. Legs AC and BC labeled. The complementary relationship ∠A+∠B=90° noted. A 3-4-5 example shown with the Pythagorean equation $3^2+4^2=5^2$.
image.caption: Right triangle: one 90° angle, hypotenuse (longest side), complementary acute angles.

classifications.value: A triangle with exactly one obtuse angle (greater than 90°). The other two angles must be acute.
characteristics: Exactly one angle $> 90°$ and $< 180°$
The other two angles are acute ($< 90°$)
The obtuse angle is the largest angle; the side opposite it is the longest side
The Pythagorean relationship gives $a^2 + b^2 < c^2$ where $c$ is the longest side
classifications.behavior: Obtuse triangles have some distinct properties: the orthocenter (intersection of altitudes) lies outside the triangle, and the circumcenter lies outside. An obtuse triangle cannot be right (by definition) or equilateral (all angles 60°).

examples.explanation:
Triangle with angles $25°$, $35°$, $120°$.

One angle ($120°$) $> 90°$ → obtuse triangle.

Sum: $25° + 35° + 120° = 180°$ ✓.

The side opposite the 120° angle is the longest side.

image.alt: An obtuse triangle with angles 25°, 35°, 120°. The 120° angle marked clearly as > 90°. The longest side (opposite 120°) highlighted. Note: 'One angle > 90° → Obtuse. Other two must be acute.'
image.caption: Obtuse triangle: exactly one angle greater than 90°.

classifications.value: A triangle with all three angles congruent. By the Triangle Angle Sum Theorem, each angle measures exactly 60°.
characteristics: All three angles $= 60°$
Equiangular $\leftrightarrow$ Equilateral — each implies the other
Also acute (all angles $< 90°$) — an equiangular triangle is a special acute triangle
Has the maximum symmetry of any triangle
classifications.behavior: An equiangular triangle is always equilateral, and vice versa. This equivalence is a key theorem. Equiangular triangles are always acute (60° < 90°), making them a subset of both the acute and equilateral categories.

examples.explanation:
If $\triangle ABC$ is equiangular: $m\angle A = m\angle B = m\angle C = 60°$.

By the Converse of the Isosceles Triangle Theorem: equal angles → equal opposite sides.

$\angle A = \angle B$ → $BC = AC$. $\angle B = \angle C$ → $AC = AB$.

Therefore $AB = BC = CA$ → equilateral.

image.alt: An equiangular triangle with all angles marked 60°. All sides marked with matching tick marks. The equivalence arrow: Equiangular ↔ Equilateral. Note: 'Also classified as acute and equilateral.'
image.caption: Equiangular triangle: all angles 60°, also equilateral and acute.

image.alt: Complete angle classification reference: four triangles with a summary table. Acute: all < 90°. Right: one = 90°, complementary acute angles, Pythagorean Theorem. Obtuse: one > 90°. Equiangular: all = 60°, also equilateral. Each with angle sum verification.
image.caption: Triangle Classification by Angles: acute, right, obtuse, and equiangular with their defining properties.

sections.introduction: Every triangle receives two independent labels—one from its side classification and one from its angle classification. Some combinations are possible (isosceles right triangle, scalene obtuse triangle), while others are logically linked (equilateral and equiangular are equivalent, and both imply acute). Understanding which combinations can and cannot occur deepens understanding of triangle properties.

introduction_image.alt: A 3×4 grid showing the intersection of side classifications (rows: scalene, isosceles, equilateral) and angle classifications (columns: acute, right, obtuse, equiangular). Possible combinations marked with green checks, impossible with red X's. Examples: isosceles acute ✓, isosceles right ✓, equilateral obtuse ✗.
introduction_image.caption: Combined classification grid: which side-angle combinations are possible?

key_concept.definition: A triangle is described by both its side and angle classifications—for example, an 'isosceles right triangle' or a 'scalene obtuse triangle.' Some combinations are impossible: an equilateral triangle cannot be right or obtuse (all angles must be 60°). A right triangle cannot be equilateral. An obtuse triangle cannot be equilateral or equiangular. The possible combinations form a logical structure based on the Triangle Angle Sum Theorem and the relationship between sides and angles.
key_concept.context: This dual classification system provides precise language for describing triangles in proofs and problems. Knowing which combinations are possible helps identify errors—if a student claims an equilateral right triangle exists, they've made a mistake because $60° \neq 90°$.

image.alt: A comprehensive grid: Rows = Side Types (scalene, isosceles, equilateral). Columns = Angle Types (acute, right, obtuse, equiangular). Cells show either a diagram of a possible triangle (green) or a red X (impossible). Notes explain why impossible combinations are impossible (e.g., 'Equilateral → all angles 60° → cannot be right or obtuse').
image.caption: Combined classification: possible and impossible combinations with explanations.

worked_examples.title: Classifying Triangles Completely
worked_examples.problem:
Classify each triangle by BOTH sides and angles. If the combination is impossible, explain why.

1) Triangle with sides $6$, $6$, $6$.
2) Triangle with angles $45°$, $45°$, $90°$.
3) Triangle with sides $5$, $7$, $10$ and angles $28°$, $42°$, $110°$.
4) Triangle with sides $3$, $3$, $5$ and angles $30°$, $75°$, $75°$.
5) Is an equilateral right triangle possible?

step_by_step.explanation:
Sides: $6 = 6 = 6$ → equilateral.
Angles: Since equilateral, all angles $= 60°$ → equiangular and acute.
Classification: Equilateral acute triangle (also equiangular).

Angles: $90°$ present → right triangle. Other two $= 45°$ → acute and complementary.
Sides: Two equal angles ($45°$) → opposite sides equal → isosceles.
Classification: Isosceles right triangle (45°-45°-90°).

Sides: $5$, $7$, $10$ — all different → scalene.
Angles: $110° > 90°$ → obtuse triangle (other two acute).
Classification: Scalene obtuse triangle.

Sides: $3$, $3$, $5$ — two equal → isosceles.
Angles: $30°$, $75°$, $75°$ — all $< 90°$ → acute. Equal angles correspond to equal sides ($75°$ opposite the $3$'s).
Classification: Isosceles acute triangle.

An equilateral triangle has all angles $= 60°$. A right triangle requires one angle $= 90°$.
$60° \neq 90°$ → a triangle cannot be both equilateral and right.
This combination is IMPOSSIBLE.

image.alt: Five diagrams: (1) Equilateral acute (60°-60°-60°). (2) Isosceles right (45°-45°-90°). (3) Scalene obtuse (110° angle). (4) Isosceles acute (75°-75°-30°). (5) Red X over 'Equilateral Right' with explanation.
image.caption: Complete triangle classification: side type + angle type for accurate description.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Equilateral acute (also equiangular).
2) Isosceles right (45°-45°-90°).
3) Scalene obtuse.
4) Isosceles acute.
5) Impossible — equilateral requires 60° angles; right requires 90°.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Dual classification by sides and angles. Understanding which combinations are possible and why.

sections.introduction: Mastery of triangle classification requires practice identifying side types from given lengths, angle types from given measures, and combining both classifications accurately. Work through these problems to build fluency with the vocabulary of triangles.

worked_examples.title: Triangle Classification Practice
worked_examples.problem:
Classify each triangle by both sides and angles.

1) Sides: $8$, $8$, $8$. Angles: $60°$, $60°$, $60°$.
2) Sides: $4$, $5$, $6$. Angles: $41°$, $56°$, $83°$.
3) Sides: $10$, $10$, $14$. Angles: $45°$, $45°$, $90°$? Check consistency. (If sides are $10$, $10$, $14$, the triangle is isosceles. The angles would be approximately $45°$, $45°$, $90°$? Actually a triangle with sides $10$, $10$, $14$ has base $14$ and legs $10$. The base angles are equal: $\cos(\text{base angle}) = \frac{7}{10}$, so base angles $\approx 45.6°$ and vertex angle $\approx 88.8°$. Not exactly 45-45-90.)
4) Sides: $7$, $24$, $25$. Angles: $16°$, $74°$, $90°$? Check: $7^2+24^2=49+576=625=25^2$ → right triangle with right angle opposite side $25$.

step_by_step.explanation:
Sides: $8=8=8$ → equilateral.
Angles: $60°=60°=60°$ → equiangular, also acute.
Classification: Equilateral equiangular (acute) triangle.

step_by_step.step: 2) All sides different, all angles acute
step_by_step.explanation:
Sides: $4$, $5$, $6$ — all different → scalene.
Angles: $41°$, $56°$, $83°$ — all $< 90°$ → acute.
Classification: Scalene acute triangle.

Sides: $10$, $10$, $14$ — two equal → isosceles.
Angles: Using trigonometry, base angles $\approx 45.6°$, vertex $\approx 88.8°$. All $< 90°$ → acute.
The given $45°$, $45°$, $90°$ is inconsistent with sides $10$, $10$, $14$ (that would be a right isosceles with base $10\sqrt{2} \approx 14.14$). Classification: Isosceles acute triangle.

Sides: $7$, $24$, $25$ — all different → scalene.
Check: $7^2 + 24^2 = 49 + 576 = 625 = 25^2$ → right triangle (Pythagorean triple).
Angles: Right angle opposite side $25$. Other two are acute and complementary ($16°+74°=90°$).
Classification: Scalene right triangle.

image.alt: Four triangles: (1) Equilateral equiangular. (2) Scalene acute. (3) Isosceles acute. (4) Scalene right (7-24-25). Each with sides and angles labeled, classification noted.
image.caption: Triangle classification practice: analyzing given sides and angles to determine complete classification.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Equilateral equiangular (acute).
2) Scalene acute.
3) Isosceles acute.
4) Scalene right.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Complete triangle classification by sides (scalene, isosceles, equilateral) and angles (acute, right, obtuse, equiangular).

summary: Triangles are classified independently by side lengths and angle measures. Key points: 1) Side Classification: Scalene (no congruent sides), Isosceles (at least two congruent sides—legs, base, vertex angle, base angles), Equilateral (all three sides congruent—a special isosceles triangle). 2) Angle Classification: Acute (all angles < 90°), Right (one 90° angle—hypotenuse opposite, legs, complementary acute angles), Obtuse (one angle > 90°), Equiangular (all angles = 60°). 3) Equivalence: Equilateral $\leftrightarrow$ Equiangular. Each implies the other. Both are always acute. 4) Impossible Combinations: Equilateral/equiangular triangles cannot be right or obtuse. A triangle can have at most one right or one obtuse angle (angle sum = 180°). 5) Dual Classification: Every triangle has a side type and an angle type (e.g., 'isosceles right triangle,' 'scalene obtuse triangle'). Some combinations are common (isosceles acute), some are special (isosceles right = 45°-45°-90°), and some are impossible (equilateral right). 6) The side and angle classifications are connected: the longest side is opposite the largest angle; equal sides are opposite equal angles.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Triangle Classification' in center. Two main branches: By Sides (scalene 0 ≅, isosceles ≥2 ≅, equilateral 3 ≅) and By Angles (acute all <90°, right one =90°, obtuse one >90°, equiangular all =60°). Sub-branches: Key Properties (isosceles base angles ≅, right: Pythagorean Theorem, equilateral ↔ equiangular). Cross-connections: Possible and impossible combinations.
review_questions_image.caption: Classifying Triangles by Sides and Angles Review: the dual classification system and their interconnections.
